questions.question: A student observes that when they start a new thread using t.start(), the thread does not immediately execute. What is the primary reason for this behavior?
options: The thread is running in the background
The thread scheduler decides when to execute the thread
The thread is waiting for a signal
The thread is in a suspended state
questions.explanation: The thread scheduler, which is part of the JVM, decides when to execute the thread based on various factors including priority and availability of processors.
hints: Consider the role of the thread scheduler
Think about how threads are managed in a multi-threaded environment
Recall the explanation of thread scheduling in the text

questions.question: If X happens, where X is a thread calling t.run() instead of t.start(), what will be the outcome?
options: A new thread is created and executed
The run method is executed in the current thread context
An exception is thrown
The thread is paused
questions.explanation: Calling t.run() directly will execute the run method in the context of the current thread, not in a new thread.
hints: Recall the difference between t.start() and t.run()
Consider the lifecycle of a thread
Think about how methods are called in Java

questions.question: In a multithreaded application, if two threads are accessing the same object's synchronized method simultaneously, what will happen?
options: Both threads will execute the method at the same time
One thread will execute the method and the other will wait
An exception will be thrown
The application will crash
questions.explanation: Only one thread will be able to execute the synchronized method at a time. The other thread will have to wait until the first thread releases the lock.
hints: Recall the purpose of the synchronized keyword
Consider how locks work in multithreading
Think about thread execution order with synchronized methods

questions.question: What will be the result if a thread calls the interrupt() method on another thread that is currently sleeping?
options: The sleeping thread will immediately stop
The sleeping thread will stop after it wakes up
The sleeping thread will ignore the interrupt
An exception will be thrown
questions.explanation: The interrupt() method sets an interrupt flag on the target thread. The sleeping thread will stop only after it wakes up and notices the interrupt flag.
hints: Recall the behavior of the interrupt() method
Consider the lifecycle of a thread in the context of interrupts
Think about how threads handle interrupts while sleeping

questions.question: If a thread calls the wait() method on an object, what is the immediate effect on the thread's state?
options: The thread enters a running state
The thread enters a waiting state
The thread enters a blocked state
The thread is terminated
questions.explanation: Calling wait() on an object causes the thread to release the lock and enter a waiting state until it receives a notify() or notifyAll() call.
hints: Recall the purpose of the wait() method
Consider the lifecycle of a thread when calling wait()
Think about thread states in synchronization

questions.question: In a producer-consumer scenario, what should the consumer thread do if the queue is empty?
options: Continue to consume items
Call wait() and enter a waiting state
Call notify() to signal the producer
Exit the program
questions.explanation: If the queue is empty, the consumer thread should call wait() to release the lock and enter a waiting state until the producer thread notifies it.
hints: Recall the producer-consumer problem scenario
Consider the role of wait() and notify() in this context
Think about how threads should handle empty queues

questions.question: What is the primary difference between notify() and notifyAll() in Java?
options: notify() notifies one thread, notifyAll() notifies all threads
notify() notifies all threads, notifyAll() notifies one thread
notify() and notifyAll() have no difference
notify() is faster than notifyAll()
questions.explanation: notify() notifies a single waiting thread, while notifyAll() notifies all waiting threads, allowing one of them to proceed.
hints: Recall the behavior of notify() and notifyAll()
Consider the difference in thread notification between the two methods
Think about how threads are woken up in a synchronized context

questions.question: If a thread is marked as a daemon thread, what will happen when the last non-daemon thread terminates?
options: The daemon thread continues to run
The daemon thread terminates automatically
The daemon thread enters a waiting state
The daemon thread is promoted to a non-daemon thread
questions.explanation: When the last non-daemon thread terminates, all daemon threads will be terminated automatically, regardless of their state.
hints: Recall the behavior of daemon threads
Consider the lifecycle of threads in a Java application
Think about the relationship between daemon and non-daemon threads

questions.question: In the context of thread groups, what is the significance of the system group?
options: It is the root group for all thread groups
It contains only daemon threads
It manages thread priorities
It handles thread synchronization
questions.explanation: The system group acts as the root for all thread groups in Java, containing system-level threads and serving as the parent for all other thread groups.
hints: Recall the role of the system group in thread management
Consider the hierarchy of thread groups
Think about the relationship between the system group and other thread groups

questions.question: What is the purpose of using the synchronized block in a method?
options: To increase the execution speed of the method
To allow multiple threads to access the method simultaneously
To ensure that only one thread can execute the block at a time
To skip the execution of the block if a thread is waiting
questions.explanation: The synchronized block ensures that only one thread can execute the block of code at a time, preventing data inconsistency issues in multithreaded environments.
hints: Recall the purpose of synchronization
Consider the impact of synchronized blocks on thread execution
Think about how synchronized blocks help in thread safety
